19.1.label: Question 19.1
19.1.scenario: A proprietary life insurance company is about to review its underwriting procedures and premium rates for its critical illness policies.
19.1.question: Outline the possible courses of action open to the company if it emerged from the investigation that the existing premium rates result in losses that, on average, have a present value equal to half an annual premium.

Possible courses of action: Make sure there are no hidden margins in the basis, ie is the contract really unprofitable?
However, the company should not simply change the basis to "make" the contract profitable.
The rest of this answer assumes the premium rates really are unprofitable. There are various risky actions when you are already making big losses.
Tighten underwriting (but this may increase costs and reduce marketability), ... or reduce underwriting (but this will almost certainly increase claim incidence rates).
Tighten claims control (but this may increase costs), ... or relax the claims control (but this will increase allowed claims).
Reduce expenses (often easier said than done).
Introduce exclusions, eg AIDS-related conditions, but this may be unpopular and such exclusions may be unworkable.
The loss may be occurring at particular ages / terms. This can be investigated using cashflow analysis.
Check experience to see that your premiums are accurate enough at different ages / terms.
Examine the rating structure for heterogeneity.
For example, if smokers and non-smokers are not distinguished, introduce non-smoker discounts.
Reinsure more, ... but the reinsurer may require all or more of the premiums.
Accept it as a "loss leader"? This may be difficult, as a loss of half a year's premium is probably unacceptable.
Increase premiums, ... but it may attract poorer risks and sell little good business.
Decrease premiums to boost business and decrease per policy costs. This could be very dangerous.
Introduce reviewable premiums and terms (or, if reviewable already, make the reviews more frequent). This allows the company to respond to changes in experience, reducing capital requirements.
However, it will be more difficult to ensure that treating customers fairly requirements are met and it may be unpopular with customers and sellers.
Pull out of that market, ... but it may experience pressure from agents to reintroduce the contract, especially if you have a direct salesforce.

19.2.label: Question 19.2
19.2.scenario: A proprietary long-term insurance company has written significant volumes of only conventional income protection business over the past few years.
19.2.question: N/A

(i).question: Explain how income protection business meets the needs of customers.

How income protection (IP) meets the nee: IP provides the insured with an income during periods of short or long-term disability, sickness or accident (referred to as incapacity).
Its function is to replace part of the income lost as a result of the incapacity.
The income lost would also be a major problem for any dependants the insured may have, eg children and spouse.
The insured can claim any number of times during the term of the policy, subject to the policy conditions.
On individual contracts, premiums are unlikely to increase with age, ... but may increase with inflation and may be reviewable depending on experience.
There are, however, limitations on the benefits, ... for example in the form of deferred periods, and in the definition of disability.
The customers of an insurer include employers.
The product may also be used to cover loan repayments (including the vehicle used to repay the loan).
Unemployment may also be a covered peril.
The benefits from an IP policy may also be used to cover insurance premium payments on other insurance policies, eg pension contributions.

Locum Protection Insurance is another form of IP. This is where professionals (eg a group of doctors in one practice) use the benefits to pay for temporary employment of a replacement for one of their colleagues in the event of that colleague's incapacity.
Group versions of IP policies are available for purchase by employers.
For employees unable to work, the employer will at least have to pay statutory sickness benefit. It may also have to pay for a replacement employee.
IP policies will protect against some of this loss and they encourage a return to work and so may help to minimise these costs.
The employer also has a moral obligation to keep its workforce happy. IP is a benefit often valued by the workforce, which may also encourage staff to work for, and remain with, the company.
It is particularly keen to keep the senior key staff happy, as they will be more valuable to the company's future, and are more difficult to replace.
IP policies will provide disability income and will also encourage a return to work via the use of benefits limited to a proportion of pre-disability income.
IP products may provide a partial benefit to those who return to work on a part-time basis or in a less strenuous role.
This will benefit the employer (by encouraging a return to work) and the employee (by helping with their recovery).
The employer may also get a tax advantage of providing IP benefits. Premiums paid by the employer qualify for tax relief, and the benefits are treated as a business expense.
The aim for the employer will be an overall cost outgo less than the benefits provided (both tangible and perceived) to all the employees.
The employer will not want the administration of the IP policies to be a burden. The assessment of claims and the provision of benefits needs to be straightforward.

Subject to keeping all levels of staff happy, the employer wants the benefits to employees to be as consistent as possible and so be seen to be fair. This would mean offering the policies to all staff, and perhaps offering a benefit consistent with salary.
The customers of an insurer include the sales staff.
The distributors of the contracts will need policies that are well-designed to meet customers' needs and with clear policy wording.
This is important since IP policies can often be complex.
A popular product will encourage cross-selling, since the sales staff would be more willing to promote other products from the same company.
The premiums and benefits should be competitive and good value for money, to ensure marketability.
The product should be reasonably flexible, giving the desired benefits. The policy could be packaged with other benefits such as PMI.
The remuneration terms (eg commission) should be satisfactory to both the distributors and the company.
The underwriting and claims process should be reasonably straightforward, not too onerous and fair to all parties so that the distributor can offer good service.
Any sales regulations requirements should be met by the product's design and packaging. For example, any literature should conform to guidelines.

(ii).question: Explain why the company will want to monitor the experience and profitability of this business on an ongoing basis.

Why monitor?: Given that there are substantial volumes of business the company needs to be aware of how the business is performing.
This is so that it can spot possible trends in order to take early action.
The key reason for monitoring is to identify divergences in actual experience from that which was assumed in the contract pricing, as part of the actuarial control cycle for the business.
The company is particularly interested in whether the contract is proving as profitable as expected, and is giving shareholders an acceptable return on their capital.
If not, the company can consider what it needs to change for existing business (to the extent it can) and for new business.
On its in-force IP business it might be able to improve its claims underwriting if that is found to be a problem.
There is little it can do on pricing if terms are guaranteed.
Either way, the company can revise assumptions in its embedded value calculations, to maintain an accurate view of the worth of the business and to recognise profits (and losses) appropriately.
If the existing contracts are reviewable, then the results can be used to change either the premiums or the benefits given.
However, the scope to do this will to an extent be limited by competitive forces and the need to treat customers fairly.
For new business it will want to use the results of monitoring to help it re-price, where this is shown to be necessary.
The information gained can be used to revise assumptions and produce new premiums.
By monitoring business volumes and mix the company can also gain some information on the marketability and competitiveness of the product by sector.
Monitoring mix is especially important if (as is likely) there is cross-subsidy between policies.

The company also needs to monitor aspects of experience such as claim inceptions and terminations, expenses and persistency in order to check the appropriateness of supervisory valuation assumptions and any regulatory reporting.

(iii).question: Give, with reasons, suitable asset classes for the company's investments. You should also indicate, with reasons, the further information you would ideally have in order to refine the asset mix.

Suitable assets: The company should invest so as to maximise return, subject to acceptable risk.

The benefit payments from the IP policies may consist of non-increasing benefits, or, perhaps more likely (particularly for group benefits), benefits that increase in line with salary inflation (perhaps limited to an absolute amount).
We therefore require further information about the mix of business and how benefits increase, ... both during the policy term and during claim payment.
Expenses can also be considered to increase in line with an index, again perhaps salary inflation.
Premiums will normally be level, but again may increase over time in accordance with the contract wording. These can be netted off the increasing benefit where applicable.
Again, more information on premiums will be needed from the company.
Assets should be in the same currency as that of the liabilities. We need to know whether any of the policies cover overseas policyholders or employees.
If there are liabilities in other countries, these could be matched with overseas investments.
For the benefits guaranteed in money terms, a mixture of suitable short-term investments would be a suitable match. For example, a variety of fixed-interest bonds and cash.
For the increasing benefits, assets that increase similarly, such as index-linked gilts, would be suitable.
An alternative would be other assets expected to give a real return, such as equities or property, ... although the volatility and marketability of these would need to be taken into account.
We should therefore like to know the size of the insurer, and its level of investment expertise to see whether these alternatives would suit.
A small company with minimal expertise would probably not want a large proportion of volatile assets.

The size of the insurer's free surplus will also have a big influence on the desirability of more risky assets, ... since a well-financed company could afford a more mismatched and risky investment approach.

As the company is a proprietary one, it has a duty to its shareholders to sustain and enhance the return it achieves on its assets backing the free surplus. This would imply some investment in equities and property.
We should also like to know what legislative restrictions there are on the investments we can make.
As the company is growing rapidly, provided it remains solvent, it can use incoming premiums to pay claims.
However, future premium income is not guaranteed and so caution should be exercised.
We should like to know the recent claims experience of the insurer. If the outgo is highly variable, a more cautious approach should be taken, retaining a high proportion of liquid assets.
Also, the extent of guarantees should be known, as a large proportion of guaranteed business may make net outgo more volatile.
Short-dated gilts and cash could be held to back the capital requirements. This would reduce the exposure to market risk and which may limit the size of any solvency capital requirement.

The need to minimise capital requirements is less important to a company with a large free surplus, so we should like to know the size of the capital requirement in comparison to the size of the free surplus.
We also need to know the tax position of the company, since this will also influence the asset mix.
The results of any ALM exercise would be useful.

19.3.label: Question 19.3
19.3.scenario:
An established life insurer markets its products through a direct sales force and through insurance intermediaries. It is now reviewing the pricing of its unit-linked critical illness product. It is intending to investigate the withdrawal experience on the company's current unit-linked critical illness portfolio. The company started writing this business eight years ago. For the first five years sales were good, but have been significantly lower over the last three years.

The critical illness benefits are paid on diagnosis of a wide variety of conditions, and the expectation is that most policyholders will ultimately claim on the policy. A withdrawal benefit is available before the end of the term, but there are no death benefits. The company also sells a traditional non-linked version of the contract, which pays benefits only on a small number of conditions, and gives no withdrawal benefits.
19.3.question: N/A

(i).question: Describe how the withdrawal experience would be analysed, mentioning any problems that may be encountered.

Analysing experience: A key variable in the analysis is duration in force.
The insurer would calculate the numbers of policies still in force at each duration as a percentage of policies originally written in the group.
It would have considerably more data for shorter durations in force than for longer ones.
It would have no data for durations above eight years.
However, longer durations are of less concern because it is usually easier to set withdrawal terms to produce an acceptable overall profit.
The insurer might consider older types of health products to give longer-duration data, but this may not be appropriate for the current type of contract.
In particular, the non-linked version of the product may give an indication of withdrawal rates.
However, the experience is likely to differ dramatically due to: no withdrawal benefit being paid; different definitions of allowable claims.
One problem is that, because of the falling volumes, the bulk of the shorter duration data is from many years ago and may no longer be relevant.
It would want to analyse the direct sales force and insurance intermediary data separately.

Other possible classifications include: premium frequency; original term of contract; size of benefit; gender; age; occupation. (However, there is unlikely to be sufficient data to split the data by all these factors.)
The insurer might also wish to study trends. Again the volume of data may be a problem, especially for recent years.
Reinsurers and consultancies may be able to help with general trends.
Alternatively, there may be industry-wide data available, although this is unlikely.

Any such trends would have to be interpreted carefully, because they may have been affected by: changes in economic conditions, including investment performance; publicity for critical illness contracts; changes in the generosity of the withdrawal benefit; changes in competition.

(ii).question: Discuss how the results of the analysis would be used to produce suitable withdrawal assumptions for use in the re-pricing.

Re-pricing: The results of the analysis would have to be used with great care, and may need considerable adjustment to be suitable for current conditions.
In particular, allowance would need to be made for the factors mentioned above (economic conditions, publicity, etc).
The company would have to take account of its expectations for the future in adjusting the past data.
It would also have to take account of any expected legislative changes, eg the tax treatment of products (although this is very difficult to predict) and commission structures.
If the company prices the product differently for the two distribution channels then it would need two sets of withdrawal assumptions.
If the prices are the same, but expenses are different, it will need an assumption for the mix of business (so overall the correct allowance for withdrawals can be made).
Because of the high level of uncertainty, the company should sensitivity test the withdrawal assumptions.
This is less necessary if the company can set withdrawal terms so that it is relatively indifferent to withdrawal rates.
Competition may limit this, and early withdrawals are always likely to lead to a loss.
